identity.years_in_role: 6 years in PM, 3 years in current role
identity.photo_description: Woman in her mid-30s with shoulder-length dark hair, wearing a navy blazer over a white blouse. Confident but approachable smile. Standing in front of a whiteboard covered with sticky notes and product roadmap sketches. Modern tech office background with natural light.

day_in_the_life.morning_routine: Sarah starts her day at 7:15am — checks Slack messages and email from her phone while her coffee brews. The overnight customer escalation from the EU team catches her eye immediately. By 7:45am she's at her standing desk, dual monitors lit up. She opens Productboard, Jira, and her personal Notion dashboard. The first 45 minutes are sacred — she reviews yesterday's feature adoption metrics and updates her stakeholder status doc before anyone else is online.
day_in_the_life.core_workday: 9am standup with her 2 engineering teams (15 min each, back-to-back). She's listening for blockers, not status — her teams self-report well. 10am-12pm is her deep work block: she's building the Q3 strategy deck for the VP review next week. She's switching between a competitor's G2 reviews, customer interview transcripts from last month, and a half-finished PRD in Google Docs. She wishes she had a tool that synthesized all of this instead of having 8 tabs open. Lunch is a 20-minute walk around the block — she processes ideas better moving.
day_in_the_life.afternoon_challenges: 1-3pm is meetings: a customer advisory call (enterprise account threatening churn because they need SSO), cross-functional alignment with Design on the new onboarding flow, and a 30-minute 1:1 with her APM who's struggling with stakeholder presentation skills. 3-4pm is firefighting: the CTO flagged a security concern about a competitor's new feature overlapping with their roadmap. Sarah needs competitive analysis fast but she knows it'll take her 2 hours to compile manually. She puts it on tomorrow's list with a sinking feeling.
day_in_the_life.evening_reflection: 5pm: Sarah closes her laptop but her mind is still running. She dictated 3 voice memos on her commute concept for tomorrow: 1) accelerate SSO feature, 2) competitive analysis for CTO, 3) prep for VP review. She knows she will spend her evening mentally structuring the deck — she wishes the AI could just build the first draft from her scattered notes. She grabs takeout, watches 20 minutes of a Netflix show, and then opens her laptop again at 8:30pm 'just to outline the deck.' She goes to bed at 11pm feeling like she worked 12 hours but only got 6 hours of deep work done.
day_in_the_life.weekly_rhythm: Monday: sprint planning + leadership sync. Tuesday/Wednesday: deep work + customer calls. Thursday: stakeholder reviews (Product Review, Design Review). Friday: 1:1s, weekly status mail, backlog grooming. Sunday evening: 30 minutes of 'getting ahead of the week' anxiety prep.

voice_samples.context: During a stakeholder review with the CTO
voice_samples.quote: I don't need the AI to tell me what to build. I need it to handle the 80% of my time I spend compiling research, formatting decks, and chasing status updates — so I can actually think about what to build.
voice_samples.tone: Frustrated but articulate — she's made this case before

voice_samples.quote: The hardest part of this job isn't the strategy. It's proving the strategy to people who need it in a completely different format. The CTO wants a technical deep-dive. The CFO wants an ROI model. The VP Sales wants a one-pager. I'm writing the same insight four different ways.

voice_samples.context: User research interview about her current workflow
voice_samples.quote: I spent 14 hours on competitive research last month. Fourteen hours. I have an MBA and 6 years of PM experience. I should be spending those hours on strategy and customer conversations, not copying-pasting from G2 reviews into a spreadsheet.

behavioral_profile.switch_cost_rationale: Her entire workflow is built on Jira/Confluence. Switching would require migrating 3 years of product history and retraining her engineering teams. Any new tool must integrate deeply with Jira or it's dead on arrival.

decision_journey.discovery_channel: ProductHunt → peer PM Slack community validation → vendor demo request

evaluation_criteria.criterion: Jira integration depth — must sync stories bidirectionally
Output quality compared to manual work — must be 80%+ parity
Security review pass (SOC2 Type II minimum)
Time-to-value: can she get useful output in first week?
Customizable output formats (her CTO wants one format, her CFO another)

deal_killers: Output quality feels 'obviously AI-generated' — must feel professionally produced

conflicts_with: VP Sales — wants every customer request prioritized immediately. Sarah wants evidence, not anecdotes.

primary.scenario: Quarterly strategy preparation: needs to synthesize 3 months of customer interviews, competitive intel, product analytics into a leadership-ready strategy deck with options and trade-offs
primary.outcome_if_solved: Strategy deck produced in 2 days instead of 2 weeks. Leadership decisions accelerated by one sprint.

secondary.scenario: Weekly status and alignment: needs to produce sprint status, flag blockers, update roadmap, and keep engineering + leadership aligned without daily meetings
secondary.outcome_if_solved: 5 fewer meetings per week. 4 hours/week saved on status compilation.

edge.scenario: Competitive emergency response: competitor launches overlapping feature. Needs competitive analysis, strategic implications, and stakeholder communication within 4 hours
edge.outcome_if_solved: Competitive response time reduced from 2 days to 4 hours. Leadership confidence in PM preparedness.

anti_use.scenario: Sarah will NOT use ProductOS as a project management tool — she already has Jira. She will NOT use ProductOS to replace customer conversations — AI can synthesize research but she will still do the interviews herself.
anti_use.outcome_if_solved: ProductOS positioned correctly as PM augmentation, not PM replacement. Sarah becomes champion, not skeptic.

must_haves: Output quality at or above what she'd produce manually in 80% of cases
Security review pass within evaluation timeline
Visible source citations on every AI-generated claim
delighters: Persona-aware output formatting (knows her CTO wants technical detail, her CFO wants financial framing)
Living system — artifacts auto-refresh when underlying data changes
One-click export to Google Slides for her stakeholder presentations
detractors: Output that looks 'obviously AI' — generic, repetitive, lacking domain-specific insight
Requires her to learn a new complex workflow — learning curve >1 hour is dealbreaker
No undo or version history — she needs to iterate without fear of losing work
indifference_zone: Mobile app — she works on desktop
Team collaboration features — she's the only PM evaluting

maturity_band_variation.zero_to_one_behavior: Early adopter. Will tolerate rough edges. Cares about speed and insight freshness more than polish. Values prototype generation more than polished PRDs.
maturity_band_variation.one_to_ten_behavior: Expects reliable output. Cares about integration depth. Needs artifacts that engineering can execute from. Values developer handoff pack more than strategy depth.
maturity_band_variation.ten_to_hundred_behavior: Expects enterprise-grade reliability. Cares about compliance and security. Needs portfolio-level views. Values dashboard and status automation more than individual artifact depth.

visual_card_spec.title: Sarah Chen — Senior PM Persona
visual_card_spec.quote_pullout: 'I need the AI to handle the 80% of my time I spend compiling, formatting, and chasing — so I can actually think about what to build.'
